questions.question_text: How many total hours of postlicensing education must a Provisional Broker complete?
questions.explanation: North Carolina requires 90 total hours of postlicensing education divided into three 30-hour courses: Post 301 (Broker Relationships), Post 302 (Contracts and Closing), and Post 303 (NC Laws/Rules/Legal). All must be completed within 18 months of licensure.

questions.question_text: A Provisional Broker was licensed 16 months ago and has completed only Post 301. She asks for an extension. What is the result?
questions.correct_answer: No extensions are granted for postlicensing
distractors: A 6-month extension is available for a fee
The BIC can request an extension on her behalf
questions.explanation: North Carolina does not grant extensions for the 18-month postlicensing deadline under any circumstances. If the Provisional Broker fails to complete all 90 hours within 18 months, her license automatically becomes inactive.

questions.question_text: A Provisional Broker completed Post 302 two and a half years ago but never finished the other courses. Is her Post 302 credit still valid?
questions.correct_answer: No, postlicensing credit is valid for only 2 years
distractors: Yes, credit is valid for 3 years
Only if she retakes the final exam portion
questions.explanation: Postlicensing course credit is valid for 2 years from the date of completion. Since 2.5 years have passed, the Post 302 credit has expired and the Provisional Broker would need to retake the course in addition to completing the remaining requirements.

questions.question_text: A broker completed her 8-hour CE course this year. Can she apply that toward her remaining postlicensing requirement?
questions.correct_answer: No, CE and postlicensing are not interchangeable
distractors: Yes, CE satisfies any education requirement
Yes, up to 4 hours can be applied
Only the mandatory Update portion counts
questions.explanation: Continuing education and postlicensing education serve different purposes and are not interchangeable in North Carolina. Postlicensing courses cover specific entry-level competencies, while CE covers updates and elective topics for experienced licensees.

questions.question_text: Which postlicensing course covers North Carolina real estate statutes and Commission rules?
questions.explanation: Post 303 covers NC Laws, Rules, and Legal concepts. Post 301 focuses on Broker Relationships, and Post 302 addresses Contracts and Closing. The mandatory CE Update course is part of continuing education, not postlicensing.

questions.question_text: How many hours of continuing education must a NC broker complete each year?
questions.explanation: North Carolina brokers must complete 8 hours of CE annually: a 4-hour mandatory Update course covering current law changes and hot topics, plus a 4-hour elective course of the broker's choosing from approved offerings.

questions.question_text: A broker completed her 4-hour elective but skipped the mandatory Update course. Has she met the CE requirement?
questions.correct_answer: No, the 4-hour Update course is required
distractors: Yes, any 8 hours of CE courses will suffice
Yes, if she takes an extra 4-hour elective
Only if she completed it by March 31
questions.explanation: The mandatory 4-hour Update course is specifically required each year and cannot be substituted with additional elective hours. Brokers must complete both the Update course and a 4-hour elective to satisfy the full 8-hour annual CE requirement.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to renew her license. When does she need to pay the renewal fee?
questions.explanation: NC real estate licenses expire on June 30 each year. Brokers must pay the renewal fee between May 15 and June 30 to maintain active status. Missing this window results in license expiration and potential reinstatement requirements.

questions.question_text: Starting April 2026, what is the license renewal fee in North Carolina?
questions.explanation: Effective April 2026, the NC license renewal fee increases from $45 to $50. Brokers must pay this fee during the May 15 to June 30 renewal window each year to keep their license current.

questions.question_text: A broker completed only 4 hours of elective CE but missed the mandatory Update course. Her license expired June 30. What must she do first to reinstate?
questions.correct_answer: Complete the missed Update course and pay fees
questions.explanation: To reinstate an expired license, the broker must complete all missed education requirements, including the mandatory Update course, and pay applicable reinstatement fees. She does not need to retake the licensing exam or redo postlicensing education.

questions.question_text: A broker on inactive status receives a referral request from a past client. Can she accept the referral fee?
questions.correct_answer: No, she must be active to perform brokerage
distractors: Yes, referrals are exempt from active status
Yes, if the referral was initiated by the client
Only if the fee is under $500
questions.explanation: A broker must hold an active license to perform any brokerage activity, including making referrals for compensation. Accepting a referral fee while on inactive status violates NC licensing law regardless of who initiated the referral.

questions.question_text: Under Rule 58A .0103, what must a broker report to the Commission?
questions.explanation: Rule 58A .0103 requires brokers to notify the Commission of changes in their name or address. This ensures the Commission can maintain current contact information for all licensees and communicate important regulatory updates.

questions.question_text: A broker is convicted of misdemeanor assault. Under Rule 58A .0113, what is her obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Report the conviction to the Commission
distractors: Only report if the crime involves fraud
Report only if she receives jail time
questions.explanation: Rule 58A .0113 requires brokers to report criminal convictions to the Commission. This obligation covers all criminal convictions, not just those involving fraud or real estate. Failure to report is itself a violation that can result in disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A brokerage firm operates three offices under common ownership. Can a single broker register with all three?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, multi-firm registration is now allowed
distractors: No, a broker may affiliate with only one firm
Only if she holds a BIC designation
Only if the offices are in the same county
questions.explanation: North Carolina now allows multi-firm registration for brokers when the firms share common ownership. This change permits a broker to be affiliated with multiple offices under the same ownership structure without needing separate licenses for each.

questions.question_text: A Provisional Broker failed to complete postlicensing within 18 months. What is her license status?
questions.explanation: Failure to complete the required 90 hours of postlicensing education within 18 months results in the Provisional Broker's license becoming inactive, not revoked or suspended. She must complete the missing education and pay reinstatement fees to return to active status.
